definition: The two halves of a price range divided by the 0.5 Fibonacci equilibrium level: premium (upper half, overvalued) favors sell setups, discount (lower half, undervalued) favors buy setups. Used to anticipate retracements and reversals, especially during consolidation, and to filter high-probability entries aligned with institutional order flow. The PD Array is a cluster of multiple structural obstacles (FVG, Order Block, Breaker Block, etc.) within these zones, often near OTE levels.
identification_rules: Identify a relevant price range using a clear swing high and swing low on a given timeframe (e.g., recent swing, London or Asia session).
Draw the 50% equilibrium level using a horizontal line or Fibonacci retracement tool.
The area above equilibrium is premium; the area below is discount.
When price extends beyond the range with a new high or low, recalculate premium/discount from the updated extremes.
When price deviates outside the range, treat equilibrium as the first magnet/target for a return back inside.

In premium zone, identify FVG, Order Block, Breaker Block, equal highs, etc. as resistance structures; in discount zone, identify FVG, Order Block, Breaker Block, equal lows, etc. as support structures.
Multiple structures densely overlapping in the same price area constitute a PD Array.
Cross-timeframe validation: after identifying PD Array on higher timeframe, drop to lower timeframe to confirm additional structures supporting the zone.
PD Array is typically located within OTE zone (Fibonacci 0.62, 0.705, 0.79); if OB open price is not in OTE, use its midpoint (50%) as alternative.
Include previous day high/low, previous week high/low, swing highs/lows as components of PD Array.
When standard deviation projections (e.g., -2 to -2.5, -4) from multiple timeframes coincide with FVG or OB, the PD Array strength is higher.
Within Mitigation Block area, drag historical order blocks from left to right on chart and map them to current price retracement zone to identify stacked support/resistance.
trading_implication: In discount zone, look for buy setups; in premium zone, look for sell setups. PD Array is a confluence of multi-timeframe structures; when FVG, OB, etc. overlap in OTE zone, it forms a high-probability entry area with superior risk-reward ratio. Price easily passing through PD Array indicates strong trend; repeated testing may signal reversal. Use Fibonacci projection targets aligned with PD Array for profit targets. In consolidation, initiate trades in premium/discount targeting equilibrium; after deviation, anticipate return to equilibrium.
common_mistakes: Looking for buy setups in premium zone or sell setups in discount zone (wrong direction).
Mistaking a single structure (e.g., single OB or FVG) for a complete PD Array, ignoring multi-layer structure combination.
Failing to align higher timeframe PD Array with lower timeframe structures, leading to unreliable entry signals.
Looking for PD Array on lower timeframe without first establishing clear displacement/MSS/CSD on higher timeframe.
Ignoring nesting relationships among multiple structures within PD Array, missing more precise entry points.
Not considering structures already swept, still treating them as valid PD Array members.
Expecting reversal after PD Array is fully swept, instead of recognizing it as trend continuation signal.
Confusing low-resistance liquidity with high-resistance liquidity within PD Array, misjudging price penetration.
Using PD Array during consolidation phase instead of expansion phase, generating false signals.
Failing to distinguish Mitigation Block from Breaker Block, leading to wrong structure for PD Array.
Confusing multiple nested ranges, unsure which PD Array to reference.
Confusing external and internal liquidity, using wrong swing points to draw range.
Focusing on a single PD Array structure instead of seeking multiple structures overlapping in same area for higher probability.
Expecting breakout or travel to opposite side after deviation, instead of rebalancing to equilibrium during consolidation.
Failing to update premium/discount zones after new high/low, using stale equilibrium levels.
Applying range-trading logic on trending/expansion days when market is not consolidating.
Assuming price will reverse at discount or premium without confirmation from other factors.
Using an incorrect or arbitrary price range for equilibrium calculation.

related_concepts.term: Change in the state of delivery

merge_notes: Both cards are from ICT school; no school conflict. The first card provides more detailed identification rules (e.g., OTE, standard deviation, Mitigation Block) and a longer list of common mistakes; the second card adds consolidation-specific usage and range update rules. Unified card merges all rules and mistakes, preserving nuance from both.
